He settled in rural Ontario, teaching school, preaching, and devoting himself quietly to serving the poor — chopping wood for widows, helping those who could not pay, giving away what little he owned. He never sought prominence. He lived among the people he served.

In 1855, hearing that his mother in Ireland was gravely ill, Scriven wrote her a poem of comfort — privately, with no thought of publication. That poem became “What a Friend We Have in Jesus,” one of the most beloved hymns in the world. When a neighbour found the handwritten manuscript during his final illness and asked who had written it, Scriven replied: “The Lord and I did it between us.”

A second fiancée died of pneumonia before they could marry. He continued to serve, teach, and give until his own death by drowning in 1886, aged sixty-six. His gravestone carries the words: “Blessed are the pure in heart for they shall see God.”

Scriven found in Christ the friend the world could not be, and spent his life pointing others to the same.

---

## Joseph Parker (1830–1902)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=joseph-parker
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joseph_Parker_(theologian)
Nationality: English · Tradition: Congregationalist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: preaching, perseverance, reconciliation

Joseph Parker was born in 1830 in Hexham, Northumberland, the son of a stonemason, with no advantages of wealth or connection. He entered Congregationalist ministry in his early twenties, serving churches in Banbury and Manchester before returning to London in 1869 to lead the historic Poultry Chapel congregation.

Parker's preaching — vivid, extemporaneous, delivered without notes — drew such crowds that his congregation built the City Temple on Holborn Viaduct, opened in 1874, one of the great nonconformist pulpits of Victorian London. He was a contemporary and, at times, public rival of Charles Haddon Spurgeon — the two sparred in print over points of doctrine and style. Yet Spurgeon later praised Parker's originality generously, and Parker was a guest of honor at Spurgeon's fiftieth birthday celebration, the two having set aside their earlier friction. Historians remember Parker as “a pillar of orthodoxy” — a description that mattered more after his death, when his own successor at City Temple embraced the theological liberalism (“New Theology”) that Parker himself had never taught.

Parker's 25-volume The People's Bible preached through the whole of Scripture verse by verse. Whatever his combative streak in argument, the substance he defended in his years at the City Temple never moved: that the Bible's authority stood, and that sinners are reconciled to God only through Christ. He died in 1902.

---

## Katharina von Bora (c. 1499–1552)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=katharina-von-bora
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Katharina_von_Bora
Nationality: German · Tradition: Lutheran · Region: Europe · Era: Reformation
Topics: grace, marriage and family, reformation, faithfulness

Katharina von Bora was born around 1499 in Saxony and, like many daughters of the minor German nobility, was placed in a convent as a child — eventually a Cistercian house at Nimbschen. Reading the reforming writings circulating out of Wittenberg convinced her and eleven other nuns that convent vows could not earn the righteousness Martin Luther preached as a free gift in Christ.

On Holy Saturday, 1523, the twelve women escaped, smuggled out hidden among herring barrels in a merchant's wagon arranged by Luther. Two years later, in 1525, Katharina married Luther himself. She ran their household in Wittenberg's former monastery — farming, brewing, and keeping boarders — freeing Luther to write and teach, and in doing so helped establish the pattern of the Protestant pastor's family still recognizable today.

Widowed in 1546, she lost much of Luther's estate to legal disputes and fled Wittenberg twice during war and plague, finally dying in 1552 from injuries sustained escaping the second time. She is remembered to have said on her deathbed, “I will stick to Christ as a burr to cloth” — the same confidence in Christ's finished work, not her own record of labor or suffering, that had carried her out of the convent thirty years before.

---

## Keith Getty (b. 1974)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=keith-getty
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keith_Getty
Nationality: Northern Irish · Tradition: Reformed evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 21st Century
Topics: hymn writing, worship, cross, grace
Hymns: In Christ Alone, The Power of the Cross, Speak, O Lord, There Is a Higher Throne

Keith Getty was born on 16 December 1974 in Lisburn, Northern Ireland, the eldest of four children of John and Helen Getty, both musicians in the Irish Presbyterian tradition. He trained classically on flute and guitar, studied at Durham University, and by his twenties had begun writing songs for his small Baptist congregation, convinced the church needed new hymns as theologically substantial as the old ones.

In 2001 he wrote the tune for "In Christ Alone," with words by English songwriter Stuart Townend — their first collaboration, and the start of what has become known as the modern hymn movement. Getty went on to co-write "The Power of the Cross" (2005) and "Speak, O Lord" (2006) with Townend, and continues to compose with his wife, Kristyn, and others through Getty Music and the annual Sing! conference. "In Christ Alone" alone is reckoned to be sung by around 100 million people worldwide each year.

When a Presbyterian Church (USA) hymnal committee asked in 2013 to print "In Christ Alone" with an altered line — replacing "the wrath of God was satisfied" with "the love of God was magnified" — Getty and Townend refused permission, and the committee excluded the song rather than change it. The line they defended is the hymn's theological center: that on the cross, God's own justice against sin was fully met in Christ, so that sinners are reconciled to him not by their own effort but by what Christ alone accomplished there.

---

## Keith Green (1953–1982)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=keith-green
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keith_Green
Nationality: American · Tradition: Evangelical (Jesus Movement) · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: gospel, grace, worship, repentance, discipleship, surrender
Hymns: Asleep in the Light, Create in Me a Clean Heart, Grace by Which I Stand, I Make My Life a Prayer to You, Oh Lord, You're Beautiful, So You Wanna Go Back to Egypt, There Is a Redeemer, Your Love Broke Through

Keith Gordon Green (1953–1982) was born in Brooklyn, New York, to a Jewish family. He showed extraordinary musical gifts from childhood — signed to ASCAP at eleven, contracted to Decca Records before his teens. But he spent his adolescence searching: drugs, Eastern mysticism, spiritual restlessness. In May 1975 he and his wife Melody attended a home Bible study in Beverly Hills and heard the gospel — that Jesus was the Jewish Messiah who had died for their sins. They believed it. Green was twenty-one.

What followed was one of the most concentrated outpourings of Christian music in the modern church. He released six studio albums, including songs that still appear in contemporary worship: There Is a Redeemer, Oh Lord You're Beautiful, Grace by Which I Stand, and Asleep in the Light. In 1977 he and Melody founded Last Days Ministries in Lindale, Texas, a communal outreach housing recovering addicts and others in need. In 1979 he decided he could not charge for his albums when the gospel itself was free — and mortgaged his home to finance distributing them without charge.

On 28 July 1982, he died in a plane crash near Garden Valley, Texas. Two of his young children, Josiah and Bethany, died with him. He was twenty-eight. In seven years of Christian faith he had called the sleeping church to wake up — and pointed it always to the Redeemer.

---

## Kenneth N. Taylor (1917–2005)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=kenneth-taylor
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kenneth_N._Taylor
Nationality: American · Tradition: Evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: Scripture, translation, children, evangelism

Kenneth Nathaniel Taylor was born on 8 May 1917 in Portland, Oregon, the son of a Presbyterian minister. He studied at Wheaton College and later trained at Dallas Theological Seminary and Northern Baptist Seminary, working for years as an editor and then as director of Moody Press in Chicago.

Taylor and his wife, Margaret, raised ten children, and each evening the family read the Bible together. Frustrated that his younger children could not follow the seventeenth-century language of the King James Version, Taylor began, in 1954, rewording New Testament passages into plain modern English — much of the work done during his daily commuter-train ride between Wheaton and his office in Chicago. What began as a private project for his own children grew, over years of revision, into a full paraphrase.

When publishers repeatedly declined the manuscript, Taylor and his wife used their own savings in 1962 to found Tyndale House Publishers and print it themselves as Living Letters, a paraphrase of the New Testament epistles. The complete Living Bible followed in 1971, becoming the best-selling book in the United States in 1972 and 1973 and eventually selling more than forty million copies worldwide.

Taylor never intended the Living Bible to replace careful study of Scripture in its original languages; he described it simply as a paraphrase meant to make the Bible's meaning clear to ordinary readers, especially children — an aim that began, and never really left, his own living room.

---

## Kristyn Getty (b. 1980)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=kristyn-getty
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kristyn_Getty
Nationality: Northern Irish · Tradition: Reformed evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 21st Century
Topics: hymn writing, worship, grace, hope
Hymns: There Is a Higher Throne

Kristyn Getty (née Lennox) was born on 22 May 1980 in Belfast, Northern Ireland, and educated at Ballyclare High School. In 2002 she was introduced to fellow Irish musician Keith Getty by her uncle, the mathematician and Christian apologist John Lennox; the two married on 16 June 2004 and have written and performed together since.

Kristyn is a hymn writer in her own right — she co-wrote "There Is a Higher Throne" with Keith in 2003, drawn from Revelation's vision of the multitude gathered before God's throne, and has since contributed lyrics to dozens more congregational songs. She is also the distinctive vocal lead on the Gettys' recordings, carrying "In Christ Alone" and their other hymns to congregations worldwide; their music is reckoned to be sung around 100 million times a year. Together the Gettys founded Getty Music and the annual Sing! conference in Nashville, aimed at restoring congregational singing to the center of church life, and in 2019 both were named Fellows of the Royal School of Church Music.

"There Is a Higher Throne" pictures every tribe and nation gathered around Christ's throne, "clothed in garments white as snow," their praise offered not on the ground of their own righteousness but because the Lamb who was slain has made them so — grace alone lifting sinners into the presence of a holy God.

---

## Leon Morris (1914–2006)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=leon-morris
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leon_Morris
Nationality: Australian · Tradition: Evangelical Anglican · Region: Oceania · Era: 20th Century
Topics: Scripture, gospel, grace, biblical scholarship, cross

Leon Morris was born in 1914 in Lithgow, New South Wales, and trained as a teacher before entering ministry, ordained a deacon in the Anglican Diocese of Sydney in 1938. Topping the national theology exam and serving first as a curate and then as a ‘bush pastor’ in outback South Australia, he went on to earn a PhD at Cambridge with a dissertation that would become one of the most significant evangelical books of the twentieth century.

That book, The Apostolic Preaching of the Cross (1955), argued — against a rising tide of theological fashion — that the New Testament presents Christ’s death as a real substitution, in which the Son bore the wrath that sinners deserved. Morris’s summary of the doctrine became famous: ‘Either we die, or He dies.’ He served as Warden of Tyndale House, Cambridge, and then for fifteen years as Principal of Ridley College, Melbourne, training generations of Australian clergy, while writing more than fifty books, including a widely used commentary on John’s Gospel, and helping translate the NIV and ESV Bibles.

For Morris, the cross was never a puzzle to be admired from a safe distance. It was the place where God himself, in Christ, paid what sinners could never pay — so that grace, not human effort, is the only ground on which anyone stands before God.

---

## Lilias Trotter (1853–1928)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=lilias-trotter
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lilias_Trotter
Nationality: British · Tradition: Interdenominational evangelical · Region: Africa · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, sacrifice, perseverance, prayer, faith, surrender

Lilias Trotter (1853–1928) was a British artist who, in her twenties, was told by art critic John Ruskin — the most celebrated critic of the Victorian age — that she had the potential to become the greatest living painter. It was not flattery: he offered to train her personally.

She declined. Converted through D.L. Moody's London meetings in the 1870s, she had concluded that no calling, however glittering, could compete with the eternal. She wrote: "I cannot give myself to painting in the way he means and continue to seek first the Kingdom of God." She served among London's poor, then in 1888 sailed to Algeria — rejected by the North Africa Mission on health grounds, she went anyway with two friends and founded what became the Algiers Mission Band.

She spent forty years among Muslim Algerians, learning Arabic, forming relationships across vast cultural distance, bearing witness over a long obedience. She never saw mass conversion. She kept painting — now as worship rather than ambition — and her devotional writings, including Parables of the Cross, remain in print. She died in Algeria in 1928. Her life poses the question she posed herself: what is more valuable — the fame that perishes, or the fruit that abides? She answered it with forty years of faithful service in a place that returned almost nothing visible.

---

## Lin Zhao 林昭 (1932–1968)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=lin-zhao
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lin_Zhao
Nationality: Chinese · Tradition: Independent evangelical (Methodist mission-school background) · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: martyrdom, persecution, suffering, perseverance

Lin Zhao was born Peng Lingzhao in 1932 in Suzhou, China. Educated at a Methodist mission school, she was baptized as a teenager, then joined an underground Communist cell during China's civil war, believing the revolution would bring justice to the poor.

What she saw instead broke that faith apart. During land reform she witnessed landlords tortured and killed; during the 1956 Hundred Flowers Movement, she watched the Party punish the very criticism it had invited. In 1957 she was branded a 'rightist' for defending open debate — and, unlike most who recanted, she refused, insisting she had done no wrong.

Arrested in 1960 and imprisoned for years, Lin turned back to the Christian faith of her youth as the only ground steady enough to stand on. Denied pen and paper, she wrote poems, letters, and prayers in her own blood, calling her sufferings an offering patterned on Christ's own sacrifice — not a way to earn favor with God, but a testimony that her life, however it ended, already belonged to the Christ who had first given his blood for her.

She was executed by firing squad in Shanghai in 1968, at thirty-six. Her family later received a bill for the bullet. Yet Lin's blood-written words survived, smuggled out by relatives and later preserved at Stanford University, still read today by Chinese Christians and dissidents alike as proof that a life surrendered to Christ cannot finally be silenced by a government's gun.

---

## Lord Shaftesbury (1801–1885)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=lord-shaftesbury
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_7th_Earl_of_Shaftesbury
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: justice, prayer, obedience, stewardship, faith, compassion

Anthony Ashley Cooper, 7th Earl of Shaftesbury (1801–1885), was one of England's most aristocratic figures who entered Parliament in 1826 and devoted sixty years there to the protection of the vulnerable. His evangelical faith, formed in youth, was the explicit foundation of everything he did. "I can do nothing without prayer," he wrote in his diary. "I begin every day of my public life with prayer."

He is best known for the Factory Acts of 1833, 1844, and 1850, which limited working hours for children in mills; the Mines Act of 1842, which prohibited children under ten and all women from working underground; and the Chimney Sweepers Act of 1875, which ended the practice of sending small boys up chimneys. He was President of the Ragged Schools Union from 1844, overseeing free education for thousands of the poorest children in Britain, and championed legislation transforming the treatment of the mentally ill.

As a child, Shaftesbury was largely unloved by his parents. His warmest memory was his family's housekeeper, Maria Millis, who read Scripture with him. When she died she left him her watch, which he wore every day for the rest of his life. When he died in 1885, the streets were lined not by lords but by the working poor, who had come to say goodbye to the man they considered their champion.

---

## Lottie Moon (1840–1912)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=lottie-moon
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lottie_Moon
Nationality: American · Tradition: Southern Baptist · Region: Asia · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, sacrifice, perseverance, faithfulness

Lottie Moon was born in 1840 to a wealthy Baptist family in Albemarle County, Virginia, and given one of the finest educations available to a woman in the South. Restless and by her own account resistant to faith, she was converted during a campus revival led by John Broadus in 1858.

In 1873, at thirty-two, she was appointed by the Foreign Mission Board and sailed for China, where she remained for nearly forty years. She taught in a girls' school at Dengzhou before moving into full-time evangelism across the villages of Shandong province. She pressed the Southern Baptist Convention to send more single women as missionaries and to fund them properly, and in 1888 the first "Christmas Offering" for foreign missions was collected at her urging — an offering still taken every year, funding missionaries around the world to this day.

Through famine, war, and isolation, Moon shared what little she had with the starving people around her, reducing herself to near-starvation in her final year. She died in December 1912 aboard a ship bound for home, her body worn out after a lifetime spent for others.

Her letters return again and again to one conviction: that Christ's command to make disciples of all nations was not a burden she carried to earn favour with God, but the grateful overflow of grace she had already, freely, received.

---

## Louis Zamperini (1917–2014)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=louis-zamperini
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Louis_Zamperini
Nationality: American · Tradition: Presbyterian / evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, forgiveness, suffering, perseverance

Louis Zamperini was born in 1917 in Olean, New York, and grew up to become a champion distance runner, competing in the 5,000 metres at the 1936 Berlin Olympics. During the Second World War he served as a bombardier in the US Army Air Forces. In May 1943 his B-24 crashed into the Pacific; he survived forty-seven days adrift on a life raft before being captured by Japanese forces. For nearly two years he was held as a prisoner of war, enduring brutal treatment, particularly under a guard named Mutsuhiro Watanabe.

Zamperini returned home from the war outwardly a hero but inwardly consumed by nightmares, rage, and alcoholism. In October 1949 he attended Billy Graham's Los Angeles crusade. Graham's preaching on the cross moved him to surrender his life to Christ, and that night his nightmares stopped for good. In 1952 he founded Victory Boys Camp to mentor troubled youth, and spent the rest of his life speaking about the faith and forgiveness that had remade him.

In 1998, at eighty years old, Zamperini returned to Japan hoping to personally forgive his former captors, including Watanabe, who refused to meet him. He forgave him anyway. Zamperini was clear that this forgiveness was not something he had willed into being through sheer determination — it was the same grace he had received at the foot of the cross, now flowing outward to men who had shown him none.

---

## Lucian Tapiedi (c. 1921–1942)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=lucian-tapiedi
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucian_Tapiedi
Nationality: Papua New Guinean · Tradition: Anglican · Region: Oceania · Era: 20th Century
Topics: martyrdom, forgiveness, missions, courage

Lucian Tapiedi was born around 1921 in Taupota village on Papua's north coast. Raised in a family under suspicion of practising sorcery, he was drawn instead to the Christian faith taught in Anglican mission schools, entering St Aidan's Teacher Training College at Dogura in 1939. By 1941 he was serving as a teacher and evangelist at the Sangara mission station, passing on the faith he had received to his own Orokaiva people.

As Japanese forces invaded Papua during the Second World War, foreign missionaries and Papuan church workers alike faced sudden danger. Tapiedi continued his work at Sangara as the invasion swept the region, one of hundreds of local Christians and missionaries who would be killed in Papua before the war's end.

On 4 January 1942, while fleeing with a small group near Kurumbo village, Tapiedi was killed with an axe by a man named Hivijapa. He was about twenty years old. The story did not end there: years later, Hivijapa himself came to faith in the same Christ that Tapiedi had died proclaiming, and built a church in his memory. A young teacher's death became, in time, the ground of his own killer's salvation — a sign that grace in Christ reaches even those who least deserve it, received by faith and not by any merit of their own. In 1998 Tapiedi's statue was placed among ten twentieth-century martyrs above the west door of Westminster Abbey.

---

## Manche Masemola (c. 1913–c. 1928)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=manche-masemola
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manche_Masemola
Nationality: South African (Pedi) · Tradition: Anglican (Community of the Resurrection mission) · Region: Africa · Era: 20th Century
Topics: martyrdom, persecution, faith, courage

Manche Masemola was born around 1913 among the Pedi people of Sekhukhuneland, in what is now Limpopo Province, South Africa. As a girl of about fourteen, she began attending catechism classes at a nearby Anglican mission alongside her cousin Lucia, preparing for baptism.

Her parents, holding to traditional religion, strongly opposed her new faith and forbade her from continuing. Manche would not give it up. Tradition holds that she said she would be baptized in her own blood if she could not be baptized in water — words that testify to a trust in Christ that did not wait for the sacrament to be genuine. Around 1928, her parents took her to a remote hillside and killed her rather than let her go on as a Christian. She died a catechumen, unbaptized, but not without faith.

Within a generation, other Pedi believers — eventually including Manche’s own mother — came to faith in the Christ she had refused to deny. The Anglican Church later recognised her as a martyr, and in 1998 a statue of her was placed among ten twentieth-century martyrs above the west door of Westminster Abbey. Pilgrims still visit her grave each year, remembering not a girl’s own courage, but the Christ who was sufficient to save her before a single drop of water ever touched her.

---

## Martin Luther (1483–1546)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=martin-luther
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martin_Luther
Nationality: German · Tradition: Lutheran · Region: Europe · Era: Reformation
Topics: Reformation, grace, Scripture, faith, suffering, gospel
Hymns: A Mighty Fortress Is Our God
Note: Luther’s later writings, especially On the Jews and Their Lies (1543), contain virulent antisemitic rhetoric that has no biblical warrant and has caused immense historical harm. These writings stand in stark contradiction to his theology of grace and represent a serious moral failure. They are condemned by Lutheran churches worldwide. His central theological contributions — sola fide, sola gratia, sola scriptura — remain foundational to Protestant Christianity.

Martin Luther was born in 1483 in Eisleben, Saxony, the son of a copper miner who sent him to study law. During a violent thunderstorm in 1505, fearing sudden death, Luther vowed to become a monk. He entered an Augustinian monastery and pursued holiness with terrifying intensity — yet found only despair. However exhaustive his penance, he could not silence the voice that told him God was against him.

The breakthrough came through Scripture. Studying Paul’s letter to the Romans, he wrestled with the phrase the righteousness of God — and finally understood it not as the righteousness that condemns, but as the righteousness given freely to the sinner through faith in Christ. “I felt myself to be reborn and to have gone through open doors into paradise,” he wrote.

On 31 October 1517 he posted ninety-five theses against the sale of indulgences — the spark that became the Protestant Reformation. Summoned before the Diet of Worms in 1521, he refused to recant unless shown by Scripture and plain reason that he was wrong: “Here I stand; I can do no other.”

He translated the New Testament into German in eleven weeks (1522), giving ordinary people direct access to Scripture. He married Katharina von Bora in 1525 and modelled a new pattern of Christian domestic life.

He died in Eisleben in February 1546 — in the town where he was born. He left behind a Reformation built on a single rediscovery: by faith alone.

---

## Martyn Lloyd-Jones (1899–1981)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=martyn-lloyd-jones
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martyn_Lloyd-Jones
Nationality: Welsh · Tradition: Calvinistic Methodist / independent evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 20th Century
Topics: Scripture, preaching, gospel, grace

David Martyn Lloyd-Jones was born in Cardiff, Wales, in 1899 and trained as a doctor, rising quickly to become chief clinical assistant to the physician who attended the royal family. But treating patients who faced death with every medical resource available and no lasting hope unsettled him. In his mid-twenties, moved by preaching he heard and by the emptiness he saw in dying rooms that medicine could not touch, he came to faith in Christ and, in 1927, gave up a highly promising medical career to become a minister.

He served first at a small Calvinistic Methodist chapel in Aberavon, Wales, and from 1938 at Westminster Chapel, London, becoming one of the century's most influential preachers. Lloyd-Jones preached for decades through entire books of the Bible — Romans, Ephesians, the Sermon on the Mount — in long, doctrinally rich sermons a colleague called ‘logic on fire,’ insisting that people needed not entertainment but the gospel itself: that sinners are justified before God by faith in Christ alone, not by their own effort. He helped found the Banner of Truth Trust and the London Theological Seminary to keep that same gospel being taught after him.

In 1966 he controversially urged evangelicals to leave denominations that no longer held that gospel clearly — a call publicly opposed by his friend John Stott. Whatever one makes of that disagreement, both men agreed on what mattered most: that the good news of grace, not the shape of any denomination, is what saves.

---

## Mary Slessor (1848–1915)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=mary-slessor
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mary_Slessor
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: United Presbyterian · Region: Africa · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, faithfulness, justice, perseverance, sacrifice

Mary Slessor was born in Aberdeen in 1848. Her father was an alcoholic shoemaker; the family moved to Dundee where Mary worked in a jute mill from age eleven, twelve-hour shifts, six days a week. Converted through the United Presbyterian Church and inspired by the death of David Livingstone, she offered herself for missionary service in West Africa.

She arrived in Old Calabar, Nigeria in 1876. She mastered the Efik language and moved progressively deeper inland than any previous missionary, eventually living among the Okoyong people far from any European presence. She wore local dress, went barefoot, and lived as her neighbours lived.

In the Calabar region, twins were believed to be cursed. One or both infants would be killed; the mother driven out. When Slessor heard the screaming she ran — sometimes barefoot through the undergrowth — to rescue the babies. She adopted many and raised them herself.

In 1892 she was appointed magistrate of the Court of Equity in Okoyong — the first woman to hold such a role in the British Empire. She died in Nigeria in 1915, having served for thirty-nine years.

A factory girl from Dundee, without formal education or social influence, who changed the culture of a region by refusing to look away from its cruelties.

---

## Matthew Henry (1662–1714)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=matthew-henry
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matthew_Henry
Nationality: British · Tradition: Presbyterian/Nonconformist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 17th Century
Topics: Scripture, exposition, prayer, devotion, grace

Matthew Henry was born in 1662 at Broad Oak Farm, Flintshire, Wales, the son of Philip Henry — a Nonconformist minister ejected from his parish that very year under the Act of Uniformity. From childhood he was formed by Scripture and by the cost of holding convictions under pressure.

Ordained in 1687, he ministered at Chester for twenty-five years, then moved to Hackney, London in 1712. He was known as a pastor who preached the whole counsel of God with warmth and loved his people faithfully.

His great work was his Exposition of the Old and New Testaments — a commentary on the entire Bible begun around 1704 and left unfinished at his death in 1714 when he fell from his horse near Nantwich. Colleagues completed the final volumes from his notes. It became the most widely read English Bible commentary ever written — not because of academic learning, but because of its devotional warmth and its consistent habit of pointing every passage to Christ.

His approach has endured for three centuries: Scripture speaks to the heart, not merely the intellect; every part of the Bible speaks of Christ; and the aim of Bible study is not to master the text but to be mastered by it. He wrote as a pastor who loved the souls before him.

The discovery that drove his life was simple: the grace of God in Christ is the beginning and end of all Scripture.

---

## Menno Simons (1496–1561)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=menno-simons
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Menno_Simons
Nationality: Dutch · Tradition: Anabaptist (Mennonite) · Region: Europe · Era: Reformation
Topics: Reformation, grace, nonresistance, discipleship
Note: Menno Simons founded a lasting Anabaptist tradition built on believer's baptism, rigorous church discipline, and mandatory nonresistance (a rejection of violence, including self-defense and military service) as conditions of church membership. These are distinctive positions not shared across evangelical traditions, including church discipline practiced as the collective “ban” — withdrawal of fellowship from the unrepentant by the whole congregation, including family members. This does not place Menno Simons outside historic Christian orthodoxy, but readers should know these positions are debated among evangelicals.

Menno Simons was born in 1496 in Witmarsum, Friesland, and ordained a Roman Catholic priest around 1515. Doubts about transubstantiation set him studying Scripture on his own, and in 1531 the execution of a man for practicing adult baptism forced him to examine what the Bible said about the rite. His crisis deepened in 1535, when his brother Pieter was killed in a violent Anabaptist uprising at Oldeklooster — a tragedy that convinced Menno the movement needed leaders committed to peace, not the sword.

On 12 January 1536, Menno renounced the priesthood and, soon after, was baptized as an adult believer. He spent the next twenty-five years as a hunted preacher across the Low Countries and northern Germany, organizing scattered Anabaptist congregations into a stable, disciplined movement — one so shaped by his leadership its members came to be called “Mennonites.” He taught that true faith “manifests itself in all righteousness and works of love... feeds the hungry; consoles the afflicted; shelters the miserable,” insisting a living faith in Christ must show itself in a transformed life.

Menno died in 1561, having spent his ministry arguing the church should follow Christ's example of nonresistance rather than the violence some radical Anabaptists had embraced. His convictions on baptism and discipline remain distinctive within the wider Christian family — see the footnote below — but his central confession matched the one held across the Reformation: sinners are saved by grace through faith in Christ, not any rite the church performs.

---

## Mitsuo Fuchida (1902–1976)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=mitsuo-fuchida
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitsuo_Fuchida
Nationality: Japanese · Tradition: Nondenominational evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, forgiveness, redemption, gospel
Note: Fuchida commanded the first wave of the December 7, 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor, in which over 2,400 Americans were killed, before his 1949 conversion to Christianity. This is noted here plainly, without minimizing the loss of life or treating his later profession of faith as erasing his role in it.

Mitsuo Fuchida was born in 1902 in Nara Prefecture, Japan, and rose through the Imperial Japanese Navy Air Service to become one of its most skilled pilots. On 7 December 1941 he commanded the first wave of aircraft against Pearl Harbor, radioing back the signal confirming surprise had been achieved. He later flew at the Battle of Midway, where he was severely wounded, and served out the war as a staff officer.

Demobilized after Japan's surrender, Fuchida carried the weight of a war that had cost so many lives. In the late 1940s he learned of Peggy Covell, an American whose missionary parents had been killed by Japanese soldiers, yet who chose to care for Japanese prisoners of war out of love for Christ. Soon after, he read the testimony of Jacob DeShazer, an American airman brutally treated as a Japanese prisoner, who had come to faith in Christ in captivity and forgiven his captors. Fuchida began reading the Bible himself — and in September 1949, he became a Christian. He spent his life as an evangelist, touring under the title 'From Pearl Harbor to Calvary,' and became a close friend of Jacob DeShazer, at times preaching alongside people his nation had once fought.

Fuchida never claimed his past could be undone by anything he did. What changed him was not remorse turned into good works, but grace — the same grace he had first seen lived out by people he had every reason to expect would hate him.

---

## Nate Saint (1923–1956)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=nate-saint
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nate_Saint
Nationality: American · Tradition: Interdenominational evangelical · Region: Latin America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, sacrifice, martyrdom, faithfulness, obedience

Nate Saint was born in 1923 near Hershey, Pennsylvania. Prevented by a childhood bone condition from completing wartime flight training, he instead put his piloting skill to use for the gospel, joining Mission Aviation Fellowship and, in 1948, opening its jungle base at Shell Mera, Ecuador, with his wife Marjorie.

Saint pioneered the 'spiraling line' technique — circling a small plane tightly enough that a bucket on a long line would hang almost motionless over one spot below — allowing him to lower gifts to isolated tribes who had never seen an outsider survive contact with them. Through these patient, prayerful gift-drops, Saint and four fellow missionaries — Ed McCully, Roger Youderian, Peter Fleming, and Jim Elliot — began building trust with the Huaorani people of the Ecuadorian rainforest, a group who had killed every outsider who had approached them before.

On 8 January 1956, having camped for days on a Curaray River sandbar in what appeared to be peaceful contact, Saint and his four companions were speared to death by Huaorani warriors. He was thirty-two.

Saint's death did not end the story. His sister Rachel Saint, and later his own son Steve, went to live among the very people who had killed him — and the gospel he had risked everything to carry took root. Christ's call to lay down one's life for others, he had believed, was not a metaphor.

---

## Nicholas Ridley (c. 1500–1555)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=nicholas-ridley
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicholas_Ridley_(martyr)
Nationality: English · Tradition: Anglican Reformer · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: Reformation
Topics: martyrdom, Scripture, grace, courage

Nicholas Ridley was born around 1500 in Northumberland and educated at Pembroke College, Cambridge, before further study at the Sorbonne in Paris. He returned to England a scholar of unusual depth, was ordained, and became chaplain to Archbishop Thomas Cranmer in 1537 — a connection that placed him at the center of England's Reformation. He served as Master of Pembroke College, then as Bishop of Rochester, and from 1550 as Bishop of London, where his preaching and writing helped establish Reformed doctrine in the English church, particularly on the nature of the Lord's Supper.

Ridley's support for the ill-fated attempt to place Lady Jane Grey on the throne made him politically vulnerable the moment Mary I secured the crown. Tried for heresy alongside Cranmer and Hugh Latimer, he was burned at the stake on 16 October 1555, outside Balliol College, Oxford, together with Latimer.

It was Ridley's own comrade Latimer, seeing him waver, who offered the words of comfort that have echoed ever since: “Be of good comfort, and play the man, Master Ridley; we shall this day light such a candle, by God's grace, in England, as I trust shall never be put out.” Ridley found the courage those words called for, dying beside his friend for a conviction he had spent his career defending — that sinners stand before God clothed in Christ's righteousness, not their own.

---

## Nicky Cruz (b. 1938)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=nicky-cruz
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicky_Cruz
Nationality: Puerto Rican · Tradition: Assemblies of God / Pentecostal · Region: Latin America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, conversion, forgiveness, redemption, evangelism
Note: Cruz has ministered within the Assemblies of God and the broader Pentecostal tradition, which holds that believers may receive a subsequent 'baptism in the Holy Spirit,' evidenced by speaking in tongues, distinct from conversion. This is a doctrinal position not shared across all evangelical traditions and is noted here for readers to weigh alongside his testimony.

Nicky Cruz was born on 6 December 1938 in Las Piedras, Puerto Rico, into a family involved in spiritism; his mother once told him he was destined to serve Satan. At fifteen he was sent to live with a brother in New York City, but he ran away and fell in with street gangs, rising within months to become warlord and later president of the Mau Maus, one of Brooklyn's most feared gangs.

In 1958, Cruz went to a rally held by a young Pentecostal preacher named David Wilkerson, who had come to the city specifically to reach gang members like him. Cruz had mocked and threatened Wilkerson before, but that night he broke down and gave his life to Christ. He trained at Bible college, served alongside Wilkerson in Teen Challenge — a ministry to addicts and gang members — and later founded his own evangelistic ministry, Nicky Cruz Outreach. He told his story in the bestselling autobiography Run Baby Run (1968), later adapted into the film The Cross and the Switchblade.

Cruz has spent more than six decades preaching in dozens of countries, always returning to the same point: that the change in his life was not something he worked up in himself. A boy raised to believe he belonged to darkness was met, instead, by a grace he had done nothing to deserve — and it was that grace, not his own resolve, that set him free.

---

## Oswald Chambers (1874–1917)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=oswald-chambers
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oswald_Chambers
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Baptist / Holiness movement, nondenominational · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 20th Century
Topics: devotion, faithfulness, Scripture, grace, service

Oswald Chambers was born in Aberdeen, Scotland, in 1874, and baptized at sixteen into a London Baptist chapel. Trained first as an artist and then in theology at Edinburgh and Dunoon, he became a gifted, searching Bible teacher, eventually founding and leading the Bible Training College in Clapham, London, from 1911 to 1915. In 1910 he married Gertrude ‘Biddy’ Hobbs, and their daughter Kathleen was born three years later.

In 1915, with the college's future uncertain amid the First World War, Chambers became a YMCA chaplain to British, Australian, and New Zealand troops stationed near Cairo, Egypt — walking among soldiers awaiting battle, teaching them the same searching faith he had taught his students. In October 1917 he underwent an emergency appendectomy; complications followed, and he died on 15 November 1917, only forty-three years old, with no book to his name.

That might have been the end of his story. Instead, his widow Biddy — who had taken careful shorthand notes of his oral talks for years — spent the following decade shaping them into a book, published in 1924 as My Utmost for His Highest. It has never been out of print since, read daily by millions in dozens of languages. Chambers himself never saw a page of it. His enduring legacy is, quite literally, not his own doing — a fitting shape for a man who taught, above all, that a Christian's usefulness rests entirely on Christ's grace, never on human achievement or length of days.

---

## Pandita Ramabai (1858–1922)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=pandita-ramabai
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pandita_Ramabai
Nationality: Indian · Tradition: Independent evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, grace, Scripture, social reform, women

Pandita Ramabai was born in 1858 into a high-caste Brahmin family in India. Her father, a Sanskrit scholar, unusually taught her to read the sacred texts — a radical act in a culture that barred women from learning. Both parents died in famine, and she became celebrated as a Sanskrit scholar in Calcutta.

After years of searching in Hinduism for relief from its oppression of women, she encountered the Gospels. She was baptised as a Christian in 1883 in England, having come to believe that Christ alone offered dignity to the low-caste, the widow, and the outcast — a conviction tested and deepened throughout her life.

She founded the Mukti ("Salvation") Mission near Pune as a refuge for child widows, famine victims, and women in poverty. In 1905 Mukti became a centre of revival — prayer and conversion spreading through hundreds of women and girls.

She also produced a Marathi translation of the Bible from the original Hebrew and Greek, completing it shortly before her death in 1922.

Her life proclaimed what the gospel insists: that in Christ there is no Brahmin and no outcaste — only the redeemed.

---

## Patrick Johnstone (b. 1938)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=patrick-johnstone
Wikipedia: https://operationworld.org/2026/01/12/gods-world-to-win/
Nationality: British · Tradition: Interdenominational evangelical · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, prayer, perseverance, faithfulness

Patrick Johnstone was born in England in 1938, the eldest of six children. Converted to Christ in 1959 during his first year at Bristol University, he abandoned a career in chemistry after hearing of the Dorothea Mission's evangelistic work in southern Africa. In 1962 he sailed for South Africa, trained at the Dorothea Mission Bible College in Pretoria, and served his first missionary term preaching the gospel in Kenya, South Africa, and Rhodesia, now Zimbabwe.

Johnstone is known worldwide as the founding author of Operation World, the prayer handbook that has shaped how evangelicals pray for the nations for sixty years. Invited to compile information for a 1964 conference, he produced a 32-page booklet on thirty countries, hand-cranked on a mission Gestetner machine, piecing together data by courier under South Africa's postal sanctions. Six editions followed, eventually covering every nation and translated into nineteen languages, mobilising prayer for unreached peoples worldwide. He went on to serve with Operation Mobilisation and WEC International's UK leadership team.

Operation World rests on a simple conviction: God graciously works through his people's prayers, though no prayer earns anything from him. Johnstone's own path, a chemistry student redirected by one missionary's testimony, later compiling statistics on hidden peoples, points to the same gospel he asked the church to pray toward: salvation belongs to the Lord, offered freely to all who trust in Christ.

---

## Peter Fleming (1928–1956)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=peter-fleming
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pete_Fleming
Nationality: American · Tradition: Plymouth Brethren · Region: Latin America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, sacrifice, martyrdom, faithfulness, gospel

Peter ('Pete') Fleming was born in 1928 in Seattle, Washington. A gifted student of philosophy at the University of Washington, where he led the university's Christian fellowship, Fleming might have pursued an academic career — but in 1952 he sailed for Ecuador instead, alongside his close friend Jim Elliot, as a missionary with the Plymouth Brethren agency Christian Missions in Many Lands.

Fleming served among the Quichua people at Shandia, learning their language in the field, and married Olive Ainslie in 1954 after Elliot encouraged him to give his first year of ministry undivided attention. Of the five men who later attempted contact with the Huaorani — Elliot, Nate Saint, Ed McCully, and Roger Youderian among them — Fleming was the only one without children of his own.

On 8 January 1956, as the group was attacked on a Curaray River sandbar, Fleming was the second to die, killed while attempting to call out friendly words in the Huaorani language.

Fleming had exchanged the promise of an academic future for a harder and more hidden calling. He had come to believe, and to live out, the truth Scripture teaches plainly: that a life is not made valuable by what it achieves, but is given its worth entirely by the Christ who redeems it through faith, not through merit.

---

## Philip Bliss (1838–1876)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=philip-bliss
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philip_Bliss
Nationality: American · Tradition: Baptist / evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: gospel, grace, perseverance, suffering
Hymns: Almost Persuaded, Wonderful Words of Life, Man of Sorrows What a Name, Let the Lower Lights Be Burning, Hallelujah, What a Savior!, I Will Sing of My Redeemer, Jesus Loves Even Me

Philip Paul Bliss was born in 1838 in Clearfield County, Pennsylvania, in rural poverty. He became a music teacher, came to faith in Christ, and later served as song leader and composer for D.L. Moody's evangelistic campaigns.

His gospel songs — Almost Persuaded, Wonderful Words of Life, Hallelujah What a Saviour — were clear, accessible, and Christ-centred. He also set to music words not his own: Horatio Spafford, who had lost all four daughters in a shipwreck, wrote, "When sorrows like sea billows roll; whatever my lot, thou hast taught me to say, it is well with my soul." Bliss's tune made them unforgettable.

Bliss and his wife died on 29 December 1876 in the Ashtabula River rail disaster, aged thirty-eight. God uses ordinary instruments for extraordinary purposes.

---

## Philip Doddridge (1702–1751)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=philip-doddridge
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philip_Doddridge
Nationality: English · Tradition: Congregationalist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: education, grace, hymn writing, revival
Hymns: O Happy Day, Hark the Glad Sound!, Awake, My Soul, Stretch Every Nerve, O God of Bethel

Philip Doddridge was born in London in 1702, the last of twenty children, and orphaned by thirteen. He was taken in by a Presbyterian minister to whom he later wrote, “To him under God I owe even myself and all my opportunities of public usefulness in the church.” He trained for ministry rather than accepting an offer to fund his studies at Cambridge, and in 1729 became pastor of an independent congregation in Northampton, where he also ran a dissenting academy that trained ministers excluded from England's universities by their nonconformity.

Doddridge wrote over four hundred hymns, among them “O Happy Day,” “Hark the Glad Sound!,” and “Awake, My Soul, Stretch Every Nerve” — hymns of vigorous, active faith that nonetheless rest entirely on grace already given rather than earned. His prose work The Rise and Progress of Religion in the Soul (1745) walked readers through conversion and Christian growth so effectively that Charles Spurgeon later called it “that holy book,” and it was translated into seven languages.

Doddridge died of tuberculosis in Lisbon in 1751, where he had traveled seeking a warmer climate for his health, and was buried in the city's British Cemetery. He left behind students trained for gospel ministry and hymns still sung by congregations who have long since forgotten his name — proof that the “opportunities of public usefulness” he received from grace, he spent giving grace away.

---

## R. A. Torrey (1856–1928)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=ra-torrey
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reuben_Archer_Torrey
Nationality: American · Tradition: Congregationalist / nondenominational evangelical · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: evangelism, prayer, fundamentals of the faith, missions, preaching

Reuben Archer Torrey was born in 1856 in Hoboken, New Jersey, and educated at Yale and Yale Divinity School before further theological study in Germany. He began as a Congregational pastor, but in 1889 was drawn into the orbit of evangelist Dwight L. Moody, becoming superintendent of the Bible institute Moody had founded in Chicago — training laypeople for ministry without requiring the university education few of them could afford.

From 1902 to 1907, Torrey led evangelistic campaigns across the English-speaking world and Asia, often alongside song leader Charles Alexander, drawing crowds that rivaled Moody's own. But his more lasting legacy was institutional rather than personal: he became founding dean of the Bible Institute of Los Angeles (BIOLA) in 1912, and from 1910 served as one of the editors of The Fundamentals — an essay series, distributed free to hundreds of thousands of pastors and workers, that defended the deity of Christ, the authority of Scripture, and salvation through Christ's atoning death against the rising theological liberalism of the day. The word “fundamentalist” itself traces back to this project.

Torrey was as insistent on prayer as on doctrine, writing extensively to urge believers to depend on the Holy Spirit rather than mere technique or personality in evangelism — a caution aimed, not least, at platform evangelists like himself. He died in 1928, having spent a working life making sure that whatever methods changed, the message underneath — Christ crucified and risen for sinners — would not.

---

## R.C. Sproul (1939–2017)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=rc-sproul
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/R._C._Sproul
Nationality: American · Tradition: Reformed/Presbyterian · Region: North America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: grace, Scripture, sovereignty of God, holiness, teaching

R.C. Sproul was born in 1939 in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. As a student at Westminster College, he was challenged by another student's plain, direct witness about Christ, and in 1957 committed his life to him. Sproul later recalled the moment as the beginning of a lifelong pursuit of knowing God more truly.

After theological study, including under G.C. Berkouwer in the Netherlands, Sproul devoted himself to teaching ordinary Christians the doctrines that had gripped him: the holiness and sovereignty of God, the authority of Scripture, and justification by faith alone. In 1971 he founded a small study centre in rural Pennsylvania that grew into Ligonier Ministries, reaching a global audience through books, radio, and recorded teaching. His best-known book, The Holiness of God (1985), retold his own realization that a right view of who God is exposes exactly how little any person could ever deserve God's favour.

In 1978 Sproul helped draft the Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy, defending the trustworthiness of Scripture as the foundation for knowing Christ rightly at all. Until his death in 2017, he insisted that sinners are not saved by their own goodness, however sincere, but by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone — the same conviction that had taken hold of him as a young student decades before.

---

## Ramez Atallah (b. 1945)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=ramez-atallah
Wikipedia: https://bethelcommunity.ca/about-us/our-missionaries/atallah
Nationality: Egyptian · Tradition: Interdenominational evangelical · Region: Africa · Era: 20th Century
Topics: Scripture, evangelism, missions, perseverance

Ramez Atallah was born in Cairo into a wealthy Christian Egyptian family. When Nasser's socialist government nationalised the family's businesses, they fled to Canada while Ramez was a teenager. Though he had attended church all his life, he had never grasped the gospel — until an evangelistic meeting in Montreal, where, as he later put it, he finally understood "the wonder of the Gospel" and gave his life to Christ. He was discipled through InterVarsity Christian Fellowship at McGill University, later directing its Quebec ministry and training at Gordon-Conwell Theological Seminary.

In 1980 Atallah returned to Egypt as IFES Regional Secretary for the Middle East, and in 1990 became General Secretary of the Bible Society of Egypt. Under his leadership the Society has grown into the largest publisher of Arabic Bibles in the world, selling Scripture at reduced prices so it stays within reach of ordinary Egyptians. After the 1992 Cairo earthquake, he had 300,000 gospel tracts distributed to a shaken city. He has also served for decades within the Lausanne Movement, including as Program Chairman for its Cape Town 2010 congress.

Atallah's own testimony is that lifelong church attendance is no substitute for grasping the gospel itself: sinners are reconciled to God by grace alone, through faith in Christ, not by heritage or religious observance. That same grace had already taken root generations earlier in his family: his paternal great-grandfather, Athanasius ("Tanassa") Ghobrial el-Assiuty (b. 1825), was a pioneer of indigenous evangelical Christianity in Egypt.

---

## Richard Wurmbrand (1909–2001)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=richard-wurmbrand
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Richard_Wurmbrand
Nationality: Romanian · Tradition: Lutheran evangelical · Region: Europe · Era: 20th Century
Topics: persecution, suffering, faithfulness, perseverance, martyrdom

Richard Wurmbrand was born in Bucharest, Romania in 1909 to a Jewish family. In his early adult years he was a Communist and an atheist. His conversion came in the 1930s through the witness of a Romanian peasant carpenter and an Anglican missionary.

During the Nazi occupation he worked clandestinely to shelter Jewish people and minister to German soldiers. When the Communists took power, he continued underground church work. On 29 February 1948 he was arrested and held for fourteen years — three in complete solitary confinement underground, subjected to systematic torture designed to break the human person entirely.

Released in 1964 and ransomed to the West, he founded Voice of the Martyrs in 1967 and wrote Tortured for Christ. He died in 2001 at ninety-one, saying that in the worst of what he had endured, Christ was present.

---

## Robert Boyle (1627–1691)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=robert-boyle
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Boyle
Nationality: Irish · Tradition: Anglican Protestant · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 17th Century
Topics: creation, faith, Scripture, prayer, stewardship

Robert Boyle (1627–1691) was an Anglo-Irish natural philosopher and one of the most important scientists of the seventeenth century. Born at Lismore Castle, Ireland, he settled in Oxford in the early 1650s and conducted systematic experiments into the properties of gases, formulating what became known as Boyle's Law. He was a founder of the Royal Society and is widely regarded as a father of modern chemistry — the first to define elements, compounds, and mixtures in terms still recognisable today.

What distinguished Boyle from many contemporaries was the depth of his Christian conviction. He was not a deist. He believed in the God of Scripture — personal, providential, revealed in Christ — and spent years learning Hebrew and Greek to read the Bible in its original languages. He financed translations of the New Testament into Irish, Turkish, and Malay, and the Old Testament into Welsh, so that more people might read it.

In The Christian Virtuoso he argued that scientific inquiry was an act of worship: the natural world was a second book written by God, and to study it was to trace the mind of the Creator. He endowed the Boyle Lectures — an annual series defending Christianity against unbelief — which continued for three centuries. Boyle demonstrated by his life the argument he made in his writing: the more carefully he examined creation, the more clearly he saw the Creator.

---

## Robert Cleaver Chapman (1803–1902)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=robert-cleaver-chapman
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Cleaver_Chapman
Nationality: British · Tradition: Open Brethren · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: love, holiness, prayer, generosity, missions

Robert Cleaver Chapman was born in 1803 in Elsinore, Denmark, to an English merchant family. Converted in his early twenties, he trained as a solicitor, gave up a promising legal career, and in 1832 settled in Barnstaple, Devon, where he would minister for the next seventy years.

He became associated with the Open Brethren and was known across the evangelical world for a rare combination of doctrinal solidity and extraordinary love. George Müller, who knew him well, called him "the saintliest man I ever knew." Men travelled to Barnstaple simply to observe how he lived. His home was open to all — he fed the hungry, housed the homeless, and corresponded with missionaries across the globe.

Chapman lived to 99 and never retired from ministry. He was a poet, an evangelist, and a letter-writer whose counsel was sought by leaders across denominations. His maxim was that love is the greatest apologetic: "Let your life preach, then let your lips, if necessary, also speak."

His life pointed to the grace that produces unhurried, unboastful, enduring love — not a love manufactured by duty, but one rooted in a sight of Christ he had been cultivating for eight decades.

---

## Robert Jermain Thomas (1840–c. 1866)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=robert-jermain-thomas
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Jermain_Thomas
Nationality: Welsh · Tradition: Congregationalist (London Missionary Society) · Region: Asia · Era: 19th Century
Topics: missions, martyrdom, Scripture, sacrifice

Robert Jermain Thomas was born in 1840 in Rhayader, Wales, the son of a Congregational minister. Ordained in 1863, he married Caroline Godfrey and sailed with the London Missionary Society for Shanghai. Tragedy met him almost at once: three months after they arrived, Caroline died from complications following a miscarriage, leaving Thomas alone at twenty-three.

Rather than return home, Thomas turned toward Korea, then almost entirely closed to Western contact. In 1865 he spent two and a half months along the Korean coast, handing out Chinese-language New Testaments — among the first substantial Scripture many Koreans had ever seen.

In 1866 he joined the American trading ship General Sherman as an interpreter, hoping the voyage would open a door for further gospel distribution. When the ship ran aground in the Taedong River near Pyongyang, tension with Korean authorities turned to violence. As the grounded vessel burned, Thomas is remembered as continuing to hand Testaments to people on the riverbank even as he was killed.

His death was not the end of his witness. Korean believers later traced the seeds of the 1907 Pyongyang Great Revival that Gil Seon-ju helped spark — the awakening that made the city known as 'the Jerusalem of the East' — back to the very Scriptures he had scattered along that river forty years before. Thomas staked his life not on his own survival but on the sufficiency of Christ's word, trusting God to bring a harvest he would never live to see.

---

## Robert Lowry (1826–1899)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=robert-lowry
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Lowry_(hymn_writer)
Nationality: American · Tradition: Baptist · Region: North America · Era: 19th Century
Topics: worship, praise, grace, hymns, gospel
Hymns: Shall We Gather at the River, Nothing But the Blood of Jesus, Christ Arose! (Low in the Grave He Lay), Marching to Zion, I Need Thee Every Hour

Robert Lowry was born in 1826 in Philadelphia. Ordained into Baptist ministry in 1854, he served a series of churches over the following decades, including Hanson Place Baptist Church in Brooklyn and, later, Park Avenue Baptist Church in Plainfield, New Jersey, alongside a season teaching rhetoric at his alma mater.

Though not formally trained in music, Lowry composed several of the era's most enduring gospel hymn tunes. In 1864, reflecting on the river of life described in the book of Revelation, he wrote 'Shall We Gather at the River' — a hymn he later dismissed, with characteristic modesty, as 'brass band music,' even as congregations everywhere kept singing it. In 1876 he wrote both words and music for 'Nothing But the Blood of Jesus,' a hymn built entirely around a single, unadorned gospel claim: that sinners are cleansed not by their own effort but solely by what Christ accomplished on the cross.

Lowry also encouraged fellow hymn writers, including Fanny Crosby, for whom he composed tunes, and Annie Hawks, whose words he set to music for 'I Need Thee Every Hour.' He died in 1899, having spent a working life reminding ordinary congregations, in the simplest possible language, that nothing recommends a sinner to God except the blood of Christ, received by faith.

---

## Robert Murray M'Cheyne (1813–1843)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=robert-murray-mcheyne
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Murray_M%27Cheyne
Nationality: Scottish · Tradition: Presbyterian · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 19th Century
Topics: holiness, prayer, revival, missions, sanctification
Hymns: When This Passing World Is Done

Robert Murray M'Cheyne was born in Edinburgh in 1813 and converted in his late teens following the death of his older brother. He studied at Edinburgh University under Thomas Chalmers, was licensed to preach in 1835, and ordained as minister of St. Peter's, Dundee, in 1836 at the age of 23.

In the seven years of his ministry he became one of the most remarkable preachers Scotland had known. His power in the pulpit was widely attributed to his extraordinary prayer life and rigorous personal holiness — he is said to have risen at five each morning to pray before studying and preaching. In 1839 he joined a delegation to Palestine to survey the prospects for Jewish mission, and on his return found that revival had broken out among his congregation in his absence.

He died in 1843, aged 29, from typhus contracted while visiting sick parishioners during an epidemic. His friend Andrew Bonar's Memoir and Remains, published shortly after, became one of the most influential books in Scottish evangelical history.

M'Cheyne's life is a rebuke and a comfort — a rebuke to the idea that long years are needed for God's purposes, and a comfort that fruitfulness flows from proximity to Christ. "Lord, make me as holy as a pardoned sinner can be" was his prayer.

---

## Roger Youderian (1924–1956)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=roger-youderian
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roger_Youderian
Nationality: American · Tradition: Baptist evangelical · Region: Latin America · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, sacrifice, martyrdom, faithfulness, conversion

Roger Youderian was born in 1924 in Sumatra, Montana. He served as a US Army paratrooper in the Second World War, fighting at the Battle of the Bulge and in Operation Varsity — and it was in England, through the influence of an army chaplain, that he professed faith in Christ in the autumn of 1944.

After the war, Youderian trained at Northwestern College in Minnesota and married Barbara Orton. In 1953 the couple arrived in Ecuador, where Youderian began missionary work among the Jivaro (Shuar) people at the remote Macuma station — a people known, like the neighbouring Huaorani, for a history of violence toward outsiders.

In January 1956, Youderian joined Nate Saint, Ed McCully, Peter Fleming, and Jim Elliot in a careful attempt at peaceful contact with the Huaorani. On 8 January, as the group was attacked on a Curaray River sandbar, Youderian was the last of the five to die, killed while trying to radio for help.

A former soldier who had once fought for his country, Youderian had come to believe that the greater battle was a spiritual one, already won at the cross — and that no one, soldier or missionary, is saved by their own courage, but only by faith in the One who fought and died for them.

---

## Rosetta Sherwood Hall (1865–1951)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=rosetta-sherwood-hall
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rosetta_Sherwood_Hall
Nationality: American · Tradition: Methodist Episcopal · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: compassion, perseverance, sacrifice, service

Rosetta Sherwood Hall was born in 1865 in Liberty, New York, and trained as a doctor at the Woman's Medical College of Pennsylvania. In 1890 she sailed to Korea as a medical missionary, and there married fellow physician William James Hall. Widowed at twenty-nine when William died of typhus caught while tending wounded soldiers, Rosetta — six weeks pregnant — briefly returned to America, then chose to go back to Korea with her small children rather than remain safely home.

Her infant daughter Edith died of dysentery soon after their return. Rather than retreat, Rosetta founded a children's medical ward in Edith's name, then went on to establish the Pyongyang School for the Deaf and Blind in 1909, pioneering Korean Braille, and in 1928 co-founded Korea's first women's medical school. She served Korea for forty-four years. Her son Sherwood Hall later became a missionary doctor himself, launching Korea's first Christmas Seals campaign against tuberculosis.

Rosetta could have called her husband's death, and her daughter's, reasons to stop. Instead she turned grief into decades of gospel-shaped service, trusting that the God who had not spared her losses was still worth giving her whole life to — not because service earns His favor, but because grace received is grace given away.

---

## Rudolf Alfred Bosshardt (1897–1993)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=rudolf-bosshardt
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rudolf_Alfred_Bosshardt
Nationality: British / Swiss · Tradition: Interdenominational evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: missions, perseverance, suffering, prayer, trust

Rudolf Alfred Bosshardt was born on 1 January 1897 in Manchester, England, to Swiss parents. Accepted for training with the China Inland Mission in 1920, he sailed for China and arrived in 1922, serving among the villages of Guizhou province. In 1931 he married fellow missionary Rose Piaget in Guiyang.

Bosshardt is remembered chiefly for what followed. On 1 October 1934, while returning from a prayer conference, he and Rose were seized by soldiers of the Communist Red Army under General Xiao Ke. Rose was released separately, but Bosshardt, together with fellow CIM missionary Arnolis Hayman, was forced to accompany the Red Army for 560 days across roughly 2,500 miles of the Long March — one of only two Westerners known to have done so for such a length. He later told the story in The Restraining Hand (1936) and The Guiding Hand: Captivity and Answered Prayer in China (1973); General Xiao Ke himself later called it the most accurate foreign account of the march.

Bosshardt was finally freed on Easter morning, 1936. Through eighteen months of forced marching, illness, and uncertainty, he clung not to his own resourcefulness but to prayer, trusting that his life lay in God's hands alone — the same undeserved grace, not earned by any effort of his own, that had first called him from England to China. He continued serving in China until expelled in 1951, then worked among Chinese communities in Laos, before settling in Manchester, where he died in 1993, aged 96.

---

## Sabina Wurmbrand (1913–2000)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=sabina-wurmbrand
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sabina_Wurmbrand
Nationality: Romanian · Tradition: Lutheran evangelical · Region: Europe · Era: 20th Century
Topics: persecution, suffering, faithfulness, perseverance

Sabina Wurmbrand (born Sabina Oster) was born in 1913 into an observant Jewish family in Czernowitz, Austria-Hungary. After studying languages in Paris, she returned to Romania, married Richard Wurmbrand in 1936, and in 1938 the couple were converted to Christ — trusting not in inherited religion or personal merit but in salvation by grace through faith in Jesus alone. Together they founded a Jewish-Christian congregation in Bucharest that grew to over a thousand converts.

When Communist rule turned against the churches, Sabina carried Scripture to Soviet soldiers and sheltered persecuted believers. She was imprisoned herself from 1951 to 1954 in a labour camp on the Danube-Black Sea Canal, leaving their young son Mihai in a friend's care. While she served her sentence, secret police staged a false account of Richard's death, hoping despair would break her; it did not. Richard remained imprisoned until 1964, when a ransom secured the family's passage to the West.

In California, Sabina and Richard founded Jesus to the Communist World, later Voice of the Martyrs, to support persecuted Christians worldwide, and in 1970 she published her own account, The Pastor's Wife. She died in 2000. She is remembered not merely as "the pastor's wife" but as a woman whose own suffering and quiet faith in Christ testified that grace sustains even when every human comfort is stripped away.

---

## Sadhu Sundar Singh (1889–c. 1929)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=sadhu-sundar-singh
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sadhu_Sundar_Singh
Nationality: Indian · Tradition: Independent evangelical · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: conversion, missions, gospel, suffering
Note: Singh's mystical experiences and some unverifiable accounts are viewed with caution by some evangelical believers. Not all reported events in his life can be corroborated, and some should be received with appropriate critical discernment. His gospel preaching was clearly Christ-centred.

Sundar Singh was born in 1889 in Rampur, in the Himalayan foothills. His mother died when he was approximately fourteen, precipitating a spiritual crisis. He became hostile to Christianity, burning pages from a New Testament.

On 18 December 1904, he arose before dawn intending to throw himself under a train. He prayed. A figure appeared — radiant, speaking in Hindustani: "How long will you persecute me? I have come to save you." He identified the figure as Jesus, and his life was transformed.

He became a sadhu — wearing the saffron robe, living on hospitality — and preached the gospel across India, Tibet, and Southeast Asia.

Note: Some accounts associated with Singh cannot be independently verified and should be received with critical discernment.

He disappeared in 1929 while attempting to cross the Himalayas into Tibet, aged approximately forty.

---

## Samuel Ajayi Crowther (c. 1809–1891)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=samuel-ajayi-crowther
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_Ajayi_Crowther
Nationality: Nigerian (Yoruba) · Tradition: Anglican (Church Missionary Society) · Region: Africa · Era: 19th Century
Topics: freedom, missions, translation, perseverance

Samuel Ajayi Crowther was born around 1809 in Osogun, in what is now Nigeria. Around age twelve he was captured during a raid, separated from his mother, and sold into slavery. The Portuguese ship carrying him was intercepted by a Royal Navy anti-slavery patrol, and he was landed at Freetown, Sierra Leone, where Church Missionary Society schools gave him an education he had never expected to receive. He was baptized in 1825.

Crowther became the first student at Fourah Bay College, was ordained, and gave himself to translating Scripture into his native Yoruba, completing the full Bible in 1884 alongside a Yoruba grammar and prayer book. In 1864 he was consecrated the first African bishop of the Anglican Church, at Canterbury Cathedral, and led the CMS Niger Mission for decades — though in his final year, younger European missionaries undermined his authority in ways historians now recognize as racially motivated.

In 1846, returning as a missionary to the region of his childhood, Crowther was reunited with his mother, twenty-five years after the raid that had torn them apart. She and other family members subsequently believed in Christ. The boy stolen from his home became, by grace alone, the means by which his own family was found by the God he had come to know in exile.

---

## Samuel Lamb 林献羔 (1924–2013)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=samuel-lamb
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_Lamb
Nationality: Chinese · Tradition: Independent evangelical (Baptist background) · Region: Asia · Era: 20th Century
Topics: perseverance, persecution, faithfulness, suffering

Samuel Lamb — known in Chinese as Lin Xiangao — was born in 1924 near Guangzhou, the son of a Baptist pastor. He trained for ministry in Hong Kong and began preaching in Guangzhou in the years before Communist rule, in the same circles as the independent evangelist Wang Ming Dao.

When China's new government demanded that every Protestant church submit to the state-controlled Three-Self Patriotic Movement, Lamb refused, insisting that Christ alone governs His church. He was imprisoned in 1955, then re-arrested in 1958 and sentenced to twenty years of hard labor, much of it spent coupling coal wagons in freezing underground mine tunnels where many men around him were killed or maimed. Released in 1979, he reopened his small house church in Guangzhou; within a few years it was drawing thousands of worshippers across several services each week, entirely without official recognition.

Looking back on the mines, Lamb said only that God had protected him though he could have died many times over. He liked to sum up his life in one line: more persecution, more growth. What carried him through two decades of imprisonment was not his own endurance but the same gift he preached every week — that God receives sinners not for their compliance or their merit, but freely, by grace, through faith in Christ alone.

---

## Samuel Medley (1738–1799)

URL: https://livesoffaith.org/person.html?id=samuel-medley
Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_Medley_(minister)
Nationality: English · Tradition: Particular Baptist · Region: UK/Ireland · Era: 18th Century
Topics: conversion, grace, perseverance, gospel
Hymns: I Know That My Redeemer Lives, Awake, My Soul, in Joyful Lays

Samuel Medley was born in 1738 in Cheshunt, Hertfordshire. Apprenticed as a teenager to an oilman, he escaped the trade by enlisting in the Royal Navy, and was severely wounded at the Battle of Lagos in 1759. Expecting to lose his injured leg, he read a sermon by Isaac Watts while recovering and was converted — the surgery, in the end, proved unnecessary, but the change in his life was permanent.

After running a school in Soho for several years, Medley joined a Baptist congregation in London, began preaching in 1766, and was ordained two years later. In 1772 he became pastor of Byrom Street Baptist Church in Liverpool, a bustling port city, where he ministered for twenty-seven years — with particular care for the sailors passing through, men who reminded him of the wounded young man he had once been. He published roughly 230 hymns, including “I Know That My Redeemer Lives” and “Awake, My Soul, in Joyful Lays,” hymns critics judged more earnest than polished but which congregations sang for generations regardless.

Medley's own testimony ran through his ministry like a refrain: a sailor who expected to lose a limb instead found a Redeemer. He spent the rest of his life telling other wounded, wandering people that the same grace that met him on a hospital bed was available to them too.

---

## Samuel Rutherford (c. 1600–1661)
